questions.q: To which notorious jail in the Andaman Islands was V. D. Savarkar transported for his anti-colonial activities?
questions.explanation: Savarkar was transported to the Cellular Jail in the Andamans.

questions.q: What political philosophy did V. D. Savarkar later articulate?
questions.explanation: Savarkar articulated the political philosophy of Hindutva.

questions.q: Savarkar authored an influential history of which great Indian uprising?
questions.explanation: Savarkar wrote a history of the 1857 revolt.

questions.q: The Cellular Jail where Savarkar was imprisoned was also known by what evocative name?
questions.explanation: The Cellular Jail was known as Kala Pani, the 'black waters'.

questions.q: Besides being a political leader, Savarkar is remembered as which of the following?
questions.explanation: Savarkar was a revolutionary, writer, and political leader.

questions.q: In which group of islands was the Cellular Jail located where Savarkar was held?
questions.explanation: The Cellular Jail was on the Andaman Islands.

questions.q: What do the initials 'V. D.' in V. D. Savarkar stand for?
questions.explanation: V. D. Savarkar's full name was Vinayak Damodar Savarkar.

questions.q: Savarkar's 1857 history called the revolt by what name in its title?
questions.explanation: Savarkar's book characterised the 1857 revolt as 'The Indian War of Independence'.

questions.q: Savarkar's revolutionary activities as a young man were directed against which power?
questions.explanation: Savarkar's revolutionary activities were directed against British colonial rule.

questions.q: Savarkar is honoured by his admirers with which title, also borne by Bal Gangadhar Tilak?
questions.explanation: Savarkar is widely referred to as 'Veer' Savarkar, meaning 'the brave'.

questions.q: In which year was V. D. Savarkar born?
questions.explanation: Savarkar was born in 1883 in Maharashtra.

questions.q: While studying in London, Savarkar was associated with which centre of Indian revolutionary activity?
questions.explanation: Savarkar was a leading figure at India House, the revolutionary hub in London run by Shyamji Krishna Varma.

questions.q: Savarkar's famous escape attempt during his transport to India occurred when he leapt from a ship at which port?
questions.explanation: Savarkar made his celebrated escape bid by jumping from the ship at Marseilles, France.

questions.q: Savarkar founded a revolutionary youth society in his early years called what?
questions.explanation: Savarkar founded the revolutionary secret society Abhinav Bharat (Young India).

questions.q: Savarkar's 1923 work that gave systematic shape to his ideology bore which title?
questions.explanation: Savarkar set out his ideology in the 1923 tract 'Hindutva: Who Is a Hindu?'

questions.q: Savarkar served for several years as president of which political organisation in the late 1930s and 1940s?
questions.explanation: Savarkar served as president of the Hindu Mahasabha.

questions.q: From which present-day Indian state did Savarkar hail?
questions.explanation: Savarkar was born in the Nashik region of Maharashtra.

questions.q: Savarkar's degree, which the British authorities effectively barred him from using after his revolutionary involvement, was in which field?
questions.explanation: Savarkar studied law in London but was denied the call to the bar because of his political activities.

questions.q: Savarkar's elder brother, Ganesh Savarkar, was also a revolutionary who was likewise sentenced to transportation. What familial role did this reflect?
options: The whole family was deeply involved in revolution
His brother was a colonial official
His brother opposed the freedom movement
questions.explanation: Savarkar's brother Ganesh (Babarao) was also a revolutionary transported by the British, reflecting the family's deep involvement.

questions.q: While imprisoned in the Cellular Jail, what kind of sentence had Savarkar received?
questions.explanation: Savarkar was sentenced to two terms of transportation for life, totalling fifty years.

questions.q: Savarkar coined the term 'Hindutva' to denote primarily what?
options: A cultural and political idea of Hindu nationhood
questions.explanation: Savarkar used 'Hindutva' to express a cultural and political conception of Hindu nationhood, distinct from religion alone.

questions.q: Savarkar was also a noted writer in which Indian language, composing poems and plays?
questions.explanation: Savarkar wrote prolifically in Marathi, including poetry and prose.

questions.q: In what year did V. D. Savarkar die?
questions.explanation: Savarkar died in 1966.

questions.q: Savarkar's escape attempt at Marseilles in 1910 led to an international legal dispute heard before which body at The Hague?
questions.explanation: The Savarkar case over his Marseilles escape was arbitrated by the Permanent Court of Arbitration at The Hague in 1911.

questions.q: Savarkar was tried as a co-accused in the 1948 assassination case of which figure, though he was ultimately acquitted?
questions.explanation: Savarkar was tried as a co-accused in the assassination of Mahatma Gandhi in 1948 but was acquitted for lack of evidence.

questions.q: Around what year was Savarkar finally released from detention and brought back to mainland India from the Andamans?
questions.explanation: Savarkar was moved from the Andamans to mainland Indian jails around 1921, and released under restrictions in 1924; he was fully freed only in 1937.

questions.q: Savarkar's first major political book, written about a European revolutionary movement and banned by the British, concerned which 1857-era inspiration drawn from abroad?
questions.explanation: Savarkar wrote a biography of the Italian nationalist Giuseppe Mazzini, drawing inspiration from the Risorgimento.

questions.q: While at India House in London, Savarkar was linked to the supply of a pistol used in the 1909 assassination of which British official?
questions.explanation: Savarkar was associated with Madan Lal Dhingra, who assassinated Sir William Curzon Wyllie in London in 1909.

questions.q: Savarkar's history of the 1857 revolt was originally written in Marathi, but its first published edition (smuggled into India after a British ban) appeared in which language?
questions.explanation: Savarkar wrote the book in Marathi in 1908, but the Marathi text was suppressed; the first published edition was the English translation, printed in the Netherlands in 1909 and smuggled into India.

questions.q: Savarkar's petitions to the British for release from the Andamans, written during his imprisonment, have been the subject of what kind of debate among historians?
options: Whether they were in a foreign language
Whether they amounted to clemency pleas compromising his stance
Whether they were addressed to the wrong authority
questions.explanation: Historians disagree over Savarkar's clemency petitions, debating whether they were tactical or a genuine softening of his revolutionary defiance.
